diagnostics.title: Array lookup inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This scales poorly because `array.indexOf()` inside a loop scans the whole list every time. Use a Set for constant-time lookups.
diagnostics.help: Use a `Set` or `Map` when you check for the same items over and over. `Array.includes`/`find` scans the whole list each time

diagnostics.message: This scales poorly because `array.indexOf()` inside a loop scans the whole list every time. Use a Set for constant-time lookups.

diagnostics.title: .map().filter(Boolean) loops twice
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .map().filter(Boolean) makes two passes, so use .flatMap() to change & drop items in one pass
diagnostics.help: Use `.flatMap(item => condition ? [value] : [])` to change and drop items in one pass, instead of building a throwaway array in between

diagnostics.title: Component defined inside another component
diagnostics.message: Your users lose all state in "AsyncFromSyncIteratorContinuation" on every render because it's defined inside "AsyncFromSyncIterator", so move it out to the top of the file.
diagnostics.help: Move it to module scope or a separate file so React does not recreate the component and erase its state on every parent render.

diagnostics.title: Static value rebuilt every render
diagnostics.message: `argumentTypes` inside `LogicalExpression` uses no local state but is rebuilt every render, so it looks new each time & breaks memoized children. Move it to the top of the file, outside the component.
diagnostics.help: Move the value above the component, at the top of the file. It doesn't use local state, so rebuilding it each update is wasted and makes it look new every time.

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .filter().map() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: Array lookup inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This scales poorly because `array.indexOf()` inside a loop scans the whole list every time. Use a Set for constant-time lookups.
diagnostics.help: Use a `Set` or `Map` when you check for the same items over and over. `Array.includes`/`find` scans the whole list each time

diagnostics.title: Children type checked at runtime
diagnostics.message: Your users hit inconsistent behavior because `typeof children === "string"` makes this component switch on what callers pass, so add clear subcomponents like `<Button.Text>` instead.
diagnostics.help: Add clear subcomponents like `<Button.Text>` and `<Button.Icon>` so callers don't have to check `typeof children`.

diagnostics.title: Pure function rebuilt every render
diagnostics.message: `mapRemainingChildren` inside `ChildReconciler` uses no local state but is rebuilt on every render, so it wastes work & breaks memoized children. Move it to the top of the file, outside the component.
diagnostics.help: Move the function above the component, at the top of the file. It doesn't use local state, so rebuilding it each update is wasted work.

diagnostics.title: Hook called conditionally
diagnostics.message: `useFiber` runs inside `updateTextNode`, which is not a component or Hook, so React cannot attach Hook state to a render.
diagnostics.help: Call hooks at the top level of a React function component or custom Hook so React sees the same hook order on every render.

diagnostics.title: Array lookup inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This scales poorly because `array.indexOf()` inside a loop scans the whole list every time. Use a Set for constant-time lookups.
diagnostics.help: Use a `Set` or `Map` when you check for the same items over and over. `Array.includes`/`find` scans the whole list each time

diagnostics.title: Children type checked at runtime
diagnostics.message: Your users hit inconsistent behavior because `typeof children === "string"` makes this component switch on what callers pass, so add clear subcomponents like `<Button.Text>` instead.
diagnostics.help: Add clear subcomponents like `<Button.Text>` and `<Button.Icon>` so callers don't have to check `typeof children`.

diagnostics.title: Children type checked at runtime

diagnostics.title: Missing effect dependencies
diagnostics.message: `useMemo`'s dependencies can't be checked because its second argument isn't an inline array, so stale values can slip through.

diagnostics.title: Missing effect dependencies
diagnostics.message: `useMemo` can run with a stale `callback` & show your users old data.

diagnostics.title: await inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This makes the for…of loop slow because each await runs one after another, so collect the independent calls & run them together with `await Promise.all(items.map(...))`
diagnostics.help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time

diagnostics.title: await inside a loop

diagnostics.title: await inside a loop
diagnostics.message: Async callback in .map runs the awaits one after another, so it is slow. Use `await Promise.all(items.map(async (item) => {...}))` to run them at the same time
diagnostics.help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time

diagnostics.title: eval() runs untrusted code strings
diagnostics.message: new Function() is a code-injection vulnerability: it builds & runs code from a string.
diagnostics.help: Use `JSON.parse` for data, or rewrite the code so it doesn't build and run code from strings.

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .filter().map() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: RegExp built inside a loop
diagnostics.message: `new RegExp()` rebuilds the pattern on every loop pass. Move it to a constant outside the loop.
diagnostics.help: Move `new RegExp(...)` (or large regex literals) to a constant outside the loop so it isn't rebuilt on every pass

diagnostics.title: Button missing explicit type

diagnostics.help: Set an explicit button `type` so plain buttons do not submit forms by accident: `type="button"`, `"submit"`, or `"reset"`.

diagnostics.help: Use a stable id from the item, like `key={item.id}` or `key={item.slug}`. Index keys break when the list reorders or filters.

diagnostics.title: Data fetching inside an effect
diagnostics.message: fetch() inside useEffect can race, double-fire, or leak. Use a data-fetching layer or Server Component instead.
diagnostics.help: Use a data-fetching layer or Server Component so fetches do not race, double-fire, or leak from `useEffect`.

diagnostics.title: State synced to a prop inside an effect
diagnostics.message: This effect adjusts state after a prop changes, so users briefly see the stale value.
diagnostics.help: Adjust the state inline during render with a `prev`-prop comparison (`if (prop !== prevProp) { setPrevProp(prop); setX(...); }`), or refactor to remove the duplicated state. Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

diagnostics.title: State synced to a prop inside an effect

Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

diagnostics.title: Multiple setState calls in one effect
diagnostics.message: 6 setState calls in one useEffect redraw your screen each time they run together.
diagnostics.help: Combine related updates in `useReducer` so one effect does not redraw the screen once per `setState` call.

diagnostics.title: Role used instead of HTML tag

diagnostics.help: Use the matching HTML element when one exists so browsers and assistive tech get native semantics.

diagnostics.title: Role used instead of HTML tag

diagnostics.title: Custom modal instead of dialog
diagnostics.message: Keyboard users can tab out of this `role="dialog"` modal because it has no built-in focus trapping, so use the native `<dialog>`, which gives you focus trapping, `Escape` to close, and the backdrop for free.
diagnostics.help: Replace the wrapper with `<dialog>` and open it with `dialog.showModal()`. For the trigger, prefer `<button commandfor="id" command="show-modal">` (Chrome 135+), or a `useRef` with `dialogRef.current?.showModal()`.

diagnostics.title: aria-activedescendant missing tabindex

diagnostics.title: Noninteractive element given interactive role

diagnostics.title: Independent awaits run sequentially

diagnostics.title: Sequential independent awaits

diagnostics.help: These two awaits don't depend on each other. Wrap them in `Promise.all([...])` so they run at the same time.

diagnostics.title: Button missing explicit type
diagnostics.message: Your users can submit the form by accident because a `<button>` with no `type` defaults to submit.
diagnostics.help: Set an explicit button `type` so plain buttons do not submit forms by accident: `type="button"`, `"submit"`, or `"reset"`.

diagnostics.title: Custom modal instead of dialog
diagnostics.message: Keyboard users can tab out of this `role="dialog"` modal because it has no built-in focus trapping, so use the native `<dialog>`, which gives you focus trapping, `Escape` to close, and the backdrop for free.
diagnostics.help: Replace the wrapper with `<dialog>` and open it with `dialog.showModal()`. For the trigger, prefer `<button commandfor="id" command="show-modal">` (Chrome 135+), or a `useRef` with `dialogRef.current?.showModal()`.

diagnostics.title: Role used instead of HTML tag
diagnostics.message: Screen reader users get more reliable semantics from `<dialog>` than `role="dialog"`, so use `<dialog>` instead.
diagnostics.help: Use the matching HTML element when one exists so browsers and assistive tech get native semantics.

diagnostics.title: Button missing explicit type
diagnostics.message: Your users can submit the form by accident because a `<button>` with no `type` defaults to submit.
diagnostics.help: Set an explicit button `type` so plain buttons do not submit forms by accident: `type="button"`, `"submit"`, or `"reset"`.

diagnostics.title: Noninteractive element given interactive role
diagnostics.message: Role `listbox` gives `<ul>` interactive semantics even though the element is noninteractive, so screen reader users get the wrong controls.
diagnostics.help: Use a real interactive element instead of adding an interactive role to a static one.

diagnostics.title: Click handler missing keyboard handler
diagnostics.message: Keyboard users can't trigger this click handler because there's no keyboard one, so add `onKeyUp`, `onKeyDown`, or `onKeyPress`.
diagnostics.help: Pair `onClick` with a key handler so keyboard users can trigger it.

diagnostics.title: Noninteractive element given interactive role
diagnostics.message: Role `option` gives `<li>` interactive semantics even though the element is noninteractive, so screen reader users get the wrong controls.
diagnostics.help: Use a real interactive element instead of adding an interactive role to a static one.

diagnostics.title: await inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This makes the for…of loop slow because each await runs one after another, so collect the independent calls & run them together with `await Promise.all(items.map(...))`
diagnostics.help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time

diagnostics.help: Remove redundant `role` attributes so assistive tech reads the element's native semantics without extra noise.

diagnostics.help: Use `.flatMap(item => condition ? [value] : [])` to change and drop items in one pass, instead of building a throwaway array in between

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .filter().map() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once
